title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 41:53-57 At last the seven years of bumper crops throughout the land of Egypt came to an end. Then the seven years of famine began, just as Joseph had predicted. The famine also struck all the surrounding countries, but throughout Egypt there was plenty of food. Eventually, however, the famine spread throughout the land of Egypt as well. And when the people cried out to Pharaoh for food, he told them, “Go to Joseph, and do whatever he tells you.” So with severe famine everywhere, Joseph opened up the storehouses and distributed grain to the Egyptians, for the famine was severe throughout the land of Egypt. And people from all around came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph because the famine was severe throughout the world. * This week has long sections of scripture that are several chapters. These verses above set the stage for the confrontation and reconciliation that will happen. For the longer sections of the context and story, refer to the readings at the top of each day.
weekTheme: Boys stay bitter, men forgive Young people today are growing up in a world filled with conflict and brokenness. Relationships are breaking down among families, friends, online, politics, and just about everywhere else. The heart of the gospel is the message of forgiveness and reconciliation. Jesus says that peacemakers will be blessed and called sons of God. This is because we become like Jesus when we make peace. This week, Joseph is going to reconcile with his brothers and forgive them. He is going to struggle to do this and test their sincerity, but in the end, a miracle will happen and his long-held vision will come to pass. This is an incredible week to highlight the beauty of the gospel and its power to bring broken relationships together.

days.title: Frustration And Forgiveness
days.scripture: GENESIS 42:1-20 1 When Jacob heard that grain was available in Egypt, he said to his sons, “Why are you standing around looking at one another? 2 I have heard there is grain in Egypt. Go down there, and buy enough grain to keep us alive. Otherwise we’ll die.” 3 So Joseph’s ten older brothers went down to Egypt to buy grain. 4 But Jacob wouldn’t let Joseph’s younger brother, Benjamin, go with them, for fear some harm might come to him. 5 So Jacob’s sons arrived in Egypt along with others to buy food, for the famine was in Canaan as well. 6 Since Joseph was governor of all Egypt and in charge of selling grain to all the people, it was to him that his brothers came. When they arrived, they bowed before him with their faces to the ground. 7 Joseph recognized his brothers instantly, but he pretended to be a stranger and spoke harshly to them. “Where are you from?” he demanded.“From the land of Canaan,” they replied. “We have come to buy food.” 8 Although Joseph recognized his brothers, they didn’t recognize him. 9 And he remembered the dreams he’d had about them many years before. He said to them, “You are spies! You have come to see how vulnerable our land has become.” 10 “No, my lord!” they exclaimed. “Your servants have simply come to buy food. 11 We are all brothers—members of the same family. We are honest men, sir! We are not spies!” 12 “Yes, you are!” Joseph insisted. “You have come to see how vulnerable our land has become.” 13 “Sir,” they said, “there are actually twelve of us. We, your servants, are all brothers, sons of a man living in the land of Canaan. Our youngest brother is back there with our father right now, and one of our brothers is no longer with us.” 14 But Joseph insisted, “As I said, you are spies! 15 This is how I will test your story. I swear by the life of Pharaoh that you will never leave Egypt unless your youngest brother comes here! 16 One of you must go and get your brother. I’ll keep the rest of you here in prison. Then we’ll find out whether or not your story is true. By the life of Pharaoh, if it turns out that you don’t have a younger brother, then I’ll know you are spies.” 17 So Joseph put them all in prison for three days. 18 On the third day Joseph said to them, “I am a God-fearing man. If you do as I say, you will live. 19 If you really are honest men, choose one of your brothers to remain in prison. The rest of you may go home with grain for your starving families. 20 But you must bring your youngest brother back to me. This will prove that you are telling the truth, and you will not die.” To this they agreed.
days.reflection: Do you find it easy or hard to forgive people? When people tick you off or frustrate you, how long do you stay mad? It’s important to know because learning how to process emotions is an important part of becoming a godly man. It’s easy to stay angry, and you may have very good reasons for being mad, but eventually, you have to show people grace and forgive them. But this can be hard. Really hard. When people hurt or wrong us, it can take a while to work through it. You are not a robot; you will have to struggle with it. Joseph did. After all the horrible years he spent in slavery and in prison, things have finally turned around. He is in charge of Egypt, has a wife and family, and things are going well. It seems like so much has been restored to him since his brothers rejected him and sold him into slavery. And then one day, out of the blue, they show up. Them. The ones who were jealous of him. The ones who lied about him. The ones who took his favored coat and threw him in a well and sold him for money and forgot about him. All the difficulties Joseph has endured are a result of their rejection, and they are bowing before him. What would you do in that moment? Would you scream at them? Would you kill them? Would you stand up and say “how do you like me now? I told you that you would bow to me.” Joseph must have been freaking out and struggling with how to respond. Then, he remembers the dream he had when he was a teenager. God told him that this would happen one day, but I bet he never thought it would happen like this. It's important to note that Joseph doesn’t just forgive his brothers on the spot in some sort of super-spiritual act of faith. He is mad, and he has to process his emotions. He is angry at what has happened to him and rightfully so. Joseph is going to have to deal with the difficulty of anger. The Bible speaks about 2 kinds of anger: Godly anger and worldly anger. Godly anger is an appropriate response to evil in the world. Even Jesus got angry. He was mad at the Pharisees. He turned over the tables in the temple when people were trying to get rich off of God. He was angry at the wrong he saw being perpetrated on others. Yet when wrong was done to him, he forgave, didn’t have a victim mindset, and even loved those who did it to him. Worldly anger is selfish, undisciplined, and seeks to pay people back for the wrong they have done to us. It makes people want to get even with those who caused them pain. It can cause bitterness and violence and heartache. It's about vengeance. The key is to not let godly anger become worldly anger. That’s why in Ephesians 4:26 it says, “'In your anger do not sin.' Do not let the sun go down while you are still angry, and do not give the devil a foothold.” It's fine to feel strong emotions when things are done to you. Yet to become a man is to learn to process those emotions and feelings. It's natural to feel frustrated, hurt, and angry, but eventually, you are going to have to learn to work through it and get to forgiveness. Joseph is going to have to, and it's going to be a real challenge for him. You will find it’s a challenge for you too, but God will help if you ask him.
questions: When you get angry, do you normally stay angry or move on pretty quickly?
Is there anything or anyone that is making you angry right now? Would you be willing to share about it?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how they handle anger. Ask them about a time they had godly anger and a time they had ungodly anger and how they could tell the difference.

thoughts.verse: Understand this, my dear brothers and sisters: You must all be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to get angry. Human anger does not produce the righteousness God desires.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you with a situation you may be angry in right now. You can vent your frustration to God. People did all throughout the Bible, especially in the Psalms. God welcomes your honest thoughts and can handle all the stuff you feel.

days.title: The Importance Of Repentance
days.scripture: GENESIS 42:21-28, 42:35-38, 43:11-14 Genesis 42:21-28 21 Speaking among themselves, they said, “Clearly we are being punished because of what we did to Joseph long ago. We saw his anguish when he pleaded for his life, but we wouldn’t listen. That’s why we’re in this trouble.” 22 “Didn’t I tell you not to sin against the boy?” Reuben asked. “But you wouldn’t listen. And now we have to answer for his blood!” 23 Of course, they didn’t know that Joseph understood them, for he had been speaking to them through an interpreter. 24 Now he turned away from them and began to weep. When he regained his composure, he spoke to them again. Then he chose Simeon from among them and had him tied up right before their eyes. 25 Joseph then ordered his servants to fill the men’s sacks with grain, but he also gave secret instructions to return each brother’s payment at the top of his sack. He also gave them supplies for their journey home. 26 So the brothers loaded their donkeys with the grain and headed for home. 27 But when they stopped for the night and one of them opened his sack to get grain for his donkey, he found his money in the top of his sack. 28 “Look!” he exclaimed to his brothers. “My money has been returned; it’s here in my sack!” Then their hearts sank. Trembling, they said to each other, “What has God done to us?” Genesis 42:35-38 35 As they emptied out their sacks, there in each man’s sack was the bag of money he had paid for the grain! The brothers and their father were terrified when they saw the bags of money. 36 Jacob exclaimed, “You are robbing me of my children! Joseph is gone! Simeon is gone! And now you want to take Benjamin, too. Everything is going against me!” 37 Then Reuben said to his father, “You may kill my two sons if I don’t bring Benjamin back to you. I’ll be responsible for him, and I promise to bring him back.” 38 But Jacob replied, “My son will not go down with you. His brother Joseph is dead, and he is all I have left. If anything should happen to him on your journey, you would send this grieving, white-haired man to his grave.” Genesis 43:11-14 11 So their father, Jacob, finally said to them, “If it can’t be avoided, then at least do this. Pack your bags with the best products of this land. Take them down to the man as gifts—balm, honey, gum, aromatic resin, pistachio nuts, and almonds. 12 Also take double the money that was put back in your sacks, as it was probably someone’s mistake. 13 Then take your brother, and go back to the man. 14 May God Almighty give you mercy as you go before the man, so that he will release Simeon and let Benjamin return. But if I must lose my children, so be it.”
days.reflection: Have you ever had someone apologize to you, but you didn’t quite believe them? It felt like they didn’t really mean it, or they had to do it to get out of trouble, or someone, like a parent, made them do it. True apologies always require sorrow for what has happened and a change of heart. The Bible calls this repentance. Repentance is a big word that gets thrown around a lot in churches, but it’s an important idea to understand if relationships are going to be repaired. Repentance simply means “a change of heart and mind that leads to a change of life.” Before Joseph can reconcile with his brothers, he wants to see if their hearts have changed. He is looking to see if repentance is present. He does this in a couple of interesting ways: When they come for grain, Joseph returns their money and hides it in their sacks. This completely freaks them out. He demands they bring the youngest son to Egypt to see him. He makes Simeon stay in Egypt in prison. It turns out, God has been at work in Joseph’s brothers’ hearts as well. Their response shows they are haunted by what they have done. Look at what they say in response to Joseph's tests: “It's because of what we did to Joseph” “What has God done to us?” Even after all these years, they still feel remorse for how they treated Joseph. They believe they have sinned in the sight of God and that He is holding them accountable for all that has happened. They are living with regret and remorse for what they have done, and they feel differently now than when they sold him into slavery. Repentance has taken root in their hearts. Repentance is the key to forgiveness. The truth is - you will be hurt by other people, and other people will hurt you. Being sorry and repenting is the key to restoring relationships. In the book of Joel 2:13, there is a really cool promise from God of what happens when we repent. It reads: “‘Don’t tear your clothing in your grief, but tear your hearts instead.’ Return to the LORD your God, for he is merciful and compassionate, slow to get angry and filled with unfailing love. He is eager to relent and not punish.” Because God is merciful and kind, he will restore relationships ruined by sin. Joseph is about to experience this, and if you walk with God, you will too.
questions: Have you ever done something wrong when you were younger and felt truly sorry for it? What was it, and what happened in your heart?
What are some of the ways that you think you can tell when someone is truly sorry?
Ask the person you are doing this study with about a time when God changed their heart and they had to repent.

thoughts.verse: People who conceal their sins will not prosper, but if they confess and turn from them, they will receive mercy.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to soften your heart and make you more sensitive to God's heart of repentance. Ask him to give you grace and to help you show grace to others.

days.title: The Cost Of Forgiveness
days.scripture: GENESIS 43:26-31 26 When Joseph came home, they gave him the gifts they had brought him, then bowed low to the ground before him. 27 After greeting them, he asked, “How is your father, the old man you spoke about? Is he still alive?” 28 “Yes,” they replied. “Our father, your servant, is alive and well.” And they bowed low again. 29 Then Joseph looked at his brother Benjamin, the son of his own mother. “Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?” Joseph asked. “May God be gracious to you, my son.” 30 Then Joseph hurried from the room because he was overcome with emotion for his brother. He went into his private room, where he broke down and wept.
days.reflection: The hardest thing about forgiving people when they have hurt us is that it doesn’t seem fair. It doesn’t seem right to let people off the hook for the wrong they have done and the damage they have caused. Joseph must have realized how much it was going to cost him to forgive his brothers for all they had done to him. In today’s reading, you can get a sense of how much he has lost. He has had to live for almost 20 years without a relationship with his brothers. And twenty years without his father’s love. He must have been grieved to know that his father suffered so much thinking he was dead when he was alive all along. This loss of relationship and the heartache and pain were the result of his brothers’ lies. When Joseph realized this, the passage tells us that he had to hurry from the room because he was overcome with emotion for his brother. Two decades of pain and loss hit him all at once and it was almost more than he could bare. Forgiveness is costly. Someone has to pay for the wrong that has been done. You can’t just wave a magic wand and make everything okay. Someone has to pay the price of the pain, the betrayal, the heartache, and the hurt. Forgiveness is a very costly act of love. Yet it’s the kind of love that men learn to show others. It cost God to forgive us. He had done nothing but loved, cared, and provided for us, yet we chose to turn our back on him. So he sent Jesus into the world to die on the cross and take away the penalty for our sins. It cost God his only son so that we could be forgiven. C.S. Lewis, who wrote The Chronicles of Narnia said this: “It costs God nothing, so far as we know, to create nice things: but to convert rebellious wills cost Him crucifixion.” God didn’t forgive us because we deserved it; he forgave us so he could have a relationship with us. And we don’t forgive others because they deserve it; we forgive them because we want our relationship with them restored. This can often be a messy process. It’s hard when people keep hurting us and keep seeking forgiveness, but we are called to do this as followers of Jesus. Peter had to wrestle with this. He asked Jesus: “'Lord, how often should I forgive someone who sins against me? Seven times?' 'No, not seven times,' Jesus replied, 'but seventy times seven!'” (Matthew 18:21-22) Imagine if God only forgave you once or ran out of patience and turned his back on you. What hope would you have? But God repeatedly shows us mercy and kindness when we don’t deserve it because he wants to be in relationship with us. We need God to give us the power to forgive when it doesn’t feel fair. The kind of power that enabled Him to love his enemies when they didn’t deserve it. The kind of love that showed you forgiveness when you didn’t deserve it. Jesus highlights this kind of love when he says in Luke 6:32-36 “If you love only those who love you, why should you get credit for that? Even sinners love those who love them! And if you do good only to those who do good to you, why should you get credit? Even sinners do that much! … Love your enemies! Do good to them… Then your reward from heaven will be very great, and you will truly be acting as children of the Most High, for he is kind to those who are unthankful and wicked. You must be compassionate, just as your Father is compassionate.” Forgiveness isn’t fair, but it's powerful and biblical. It's costly but worth paying to see relationships restored.
questions: Do you think forgiveness is fair? Why or why not?
Why can’t God just say that we are forgiven? Why must someone pay the price?
What is the most costly thing you have ever done to forgive someone?

thoughts.verse: Love prospers when a fault is forgiven, but dwelling on it separates close friends.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a fresh picture of his costly love. Ask him to show you his mercy in a new light today so that you may pass it on to others.

days.title: A Tender Heart
days.scripture: GENESIS 41:33-36 19 The brothers approached the manager of Joseph’s household and spoke to him at the entrance to the palace. 20 “Sir,” they said, “we came to Egypt once before to buy food. 21 But as we were returning home, we stopped for the night and opened our sacks. Then we discovered that each man’s money—the exact amount paid—was in the top of his sack! Here it is; we have brought it back with us. 22 We also have additional money to buy more food. We have no idea who put our money in our sacks.” 23 “Relax. Don’t be afraid,” the household manager told them. “Your God, the God of your father, must have put this treasure into your sacks. I know I received your payment.” Then he released Simeon and brought him out to them. 24 The manager then led the men into Joseph’s palace. He gave them water to wash their feet and provided food for their donkeys. 25 They were told they would be eating there, so they prepared their gifts for Joseph’s arrival at noon. 26 When Joseph came home, they gave him the gifts they had brought him, then bowed low to the ground before him. 27 After greeting them, he asked, “How is your father, the old man you spoke about? Is he still alive?” 28 “Yes,” they replied. “Our father, your servant, is alive and well.” And they bowed low again. 29 Then Joseph looked at his brother Benjamin, the son of his own mother. “Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?” Joseph asked. “May God be gracious to you, my son.” 30 Then Joseph hurried from the room because he was overcome with emotion for his brother. He went into his private room, where he broke down and wept. 31 After washing his face, he came back out, keeping himself under control. Then he ordered, “Bring out the food!” 32 The waiters served Joseph at his own table, and his brothers were served at a separate table. The Egyptians who ate with Joseph sat at their own table, because Egyptians despise Hebrews and refuse to eat with them. 33 Joseph told each of his brothers where to sit, and to their amazement, he seated them according to age, from oldest to youngest. 34 And Joseph filled their plates with food from his own table, giving Benjamin five times as much as he gave the others. So they feasted and drank freely with him.
days.reflection: After all that Joseph has been through, he is going to face his toughest test yet: spending time with his brothers face-to-face over a meal. You have probably noticed by now that when there is conflict between people, getting together can be really awkward. It feels really weird being in the room with someone you have had a fight with. There is a huge unspoken tension where you want to say a million things but you don’t know where to start. Have you ever experienced that? Joseph must have wanted to say a thousand things to his brothers. Scream at them, hug them, find out what has happened in their lives, wonder about his life back home. We can see that there is tension in his heart. The tension of wanting to forgive and the tension of wanting to check their hearts. The tension of wanting to reveal himself to them and the tension of making them come to terms with what they did to him. And all these tensions are touching his heart in a deep place. Joseph is trying to hold it all together (remember they still don’t know who he is but he knows who they are), but slowly his anger is melting. His heart is becoming tender. Joseph is moving towards his brothers. One thing to note about Joseph is all of his tears. He feels deeply and weeps often. Take a look at all the places this happens in his life: Genesis 42:24 “Now he turned away from them and began to weep. When he regained his composure, he spoke to them again.” Genesis 45:2 “Then he broke down and wept. He wept so loudly the Egyptians could hear him, and word of it quickly carried to Pharaoh’s palace.” Genesis 45:14-15 “Then Joseph kissed each of his brothers and wept over them, and after that they began talking freely with him.” Genesis 50:17 "When Joseph received the message, he broke down and wept." Wrestling with forgiveness and repairing our relationships is hard. It touches us in a deep place. It moves us to the core and can be really costly. Do you ever cry when you get hurt? Joseph did. This shows us that strong men do cry, feel deeply, and wrestle to manage their feelings when they have been hurt. This is a natural part of the process of forgiveness and reconciliation. However, if you are a Christian, it’s hard to stay mad at someone forever. It’s hard to remain bitter no matter what someone has done to you. This is because God has put his love in our hearts, and this love is based on forgiveness. This love is based on Jesus showing us mercy when we have been sinful and selfish. It's based on Jesus dying to take away the penalty of our sins so that we can be made right with God. That’s why Paul wrote in Ephesians 4:31-32: “Get rid of all bitterness, rage, anger, harsh words, and slander, as well as all types of evil behavior. Instead, be kind to each other, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, just as God through Christ has forgiven you.” God has shown you grace; show it to others. God has shown you mercy; show it to others. God has shown you kindness; show it to others. Psalms 86:5 Says, “O Lord, you are so good, so ready to forgive, so full of unfailing love for all who ask for your help.” God’s love is unfailing. It doesn’t break. It doesn’t stop. He helps us when we need forgiveness, and he helps us forgive others. He can help you today if you ask him.
questions: Why do you think men feel like they cannot cry when they are hurt? Have you ever seen a grown man cry?
Have you ever cried when someone hurt your feelings or did something painful to you? Would you be willing to share about it?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about a time they were hurt and had to learn to forgive someone. Ask them how their heart changed over time.

thoughts.verse: “If you forgive those who sin against you, your heavenly Father will forgive you. But if you refuse to forgive others, your Father will not forgive your sins.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to soften your heart towards anyone who may have hurt you. Ask him for a fresh understanding of how kind he has been to you and how you can show that to others.

days.title: The Beauty Of Reconciliation
days.scripture: GENESIS 45:1-15 1 Joseph could stand it no longer. There were many people in the room, and he said to his attendants, “Out, all of you!” So he was alone with his brothers when he told them who he was. 2 Then he broke down and wept. He wept so loudly the Egyptians could hear him, and word of it quickly carried to Pharaoh’s palace. 3 “I am Joseph!” he said to his brothers. “Is my father still alive?” But his brothers were speechless! They were stunned to realize that Joseph was standing there in front of them. 4 “Please, come closer,” he said to them. So they came closer. And he said again, “I am Joseph, your brother, whom you sold into slavery in Egypt. 5 But don’t be upset, and don’t be angry with yourselves for selling me to this place. It was God who sent me here ahead of you to preserve your lives. 6 This famine that has ravaged the land for two years will last five more years, and there will be neither plowing nor harvesting. 7 God has sent me ahead of you to keep you and your families alive and to preserve many survivors. 8 So it was God who sent me here, not you! And he is the one who made me an adviser to Pharaoh—the manager of his entire palace and the governor of all Egypt. 9 “Now hurry back to my father and tell him, ‘This is what your son Joseph says: God has made me master over all the land of Egypt. So come down to me immediately! 10 You can live in the region of Goshen, where you can be near me with all your children and grandchildren, your flocks and herds, and everything you own. 11 I will take care of you there, for there are still five years of famine ahead of us. Otherwise you, your household, and all your animals will starve.’” 12 Then Joseph added, “Look! You can see for yourselves, and so can my brother Benjamin, that I really am Joseph! 13 Go tell my father of my honored position here in Egypt. Describe for him everything you have seen, and then bring my father here quickly.” 14 Weeping with joy, he embraced Benjamin, and Benjamin did the same. 15 Then Joseph kissed each of his brothers and wept over them, and after that they began talking freely with him.
days.reflection: Gwen Smith says this: “When we allow God’s love to trump our anger, we are able to experience restoration in relationships.” That’s a pretty powerful phrase right there: God’s love trumps human anger. This is what has happened in Joseph's heart. And it’s a real miracle that this has happened. Joseph has every right to be bitter at his brothers. Every right to get even with them. Every right to pay them back. And he not only has the right to do this, but he also has the power to do it. He could unleash his fury on them, starve them out, and send them home with guilt and shame to tell their father what they really did. But by God’s grace, this doesn’t happen. Something has changed in Joseph's heart. He doesn’t feel anger; he feels love. He has wrestled with his emotions and been honest with his pain. He has tested his brothers’ hearts to see if there is true repentance. He has felt God soften his heart towards them in supernatural ways. And now he can stand it no longer. He bursts forth with emotion into a powerful moment of reconciliation. This has to have freaked the brothers out. Today’s passage says “they were stunned to see Joseph standing in front of them.” They must have been filled with absolute terror. They probably had thoughts like these rushing through their minds: “How is this possible? What is he going to do to us? How are we going to escape his wrath?” Yet the greatest shock to come wasn’t that it was Joseph in front of them, the second most powerful man in Egypt. It was that standing in front of them was Joseph, the most forgiving man in Egypt. What he said shows a maturity that is hard to comprehend: “I am Joseph, your brother, whom you sold into slavery in Egypt. But don’t be upset, and don’t be angry with yourselves for selling me to this place. It was God who sent me here ahead of you to preserve your lives…. God has sent me ahead of you to keep you and your families alive and to preserve many survivors. So it was God who sent me here, not you! “ Don’t be upset. Don’t be angry with yourself. God sent me ahead of you and he had a plan all along. was sold into slavery, lied about, betrayed, and forgotten, he never lost heart. He was responsible in Potiphar’s house, and it led to his promotion. He was responsible in prison, and it led to his promotion. And now he is going to be rewarded for accepting responsibility to lead the land. The ability to see things from God's perspective and not just a human one is the difference between a worldly boy and a godly man. Joseph doesn’t feel like a victim of his circumstances but a servant of God. He can see how God was working all along to position him for his dream to come true and to save the lives of many people. Psalms 37:23-24 says. “The LORD directs the steps of the godly. He delights in every detail of their lives. Though they stumble, they will never fall, for the LORD holds them by the hand.” Joseph learned to see the hand of God in the most horrible circumstances and discern the ways he was at work to bring things together for good. This takes real maturity, divine help, and deep faith. As you grow up, you are going to have to learn to trust God too. Even when things are hard, relationships are strained, and things aren’t going your way, you can be sure that God will work this out for your good and the benefit of others. It may take some time, but just you wait and see. It is God, not fate or chance, directing your life.
questions: Life would seem much easier if God would just snap his fingers and make everyone forgive one another and get along. Why do you think he chooses not to do that?
Have you ever been really mad at someone but then felt your heart soften and change towards them? Were you able to mend that relationship?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a story of when they were able to reconcile a broken relationship they had. Ask them to share how the relationship was broken and what happened to make forgiveness and restoration possible.

thoughts.verse: Make allowance for each other’s faults, and forgive anyone who offends you. Remember, the Lord forgave you, so you must forgive others. Above all, clothe yourselves with love, which binds us all together in perfect harmony.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help heal any broken relationships you may be dealing with right now. Ask him to change your heart, change the person's heart, and show you both God's love.

extras: WEEK 8 MANSCHOOL WHITE SHIRTS AND WINGS MANSCHOOL REMINDER: "The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner." John Eldredge OPTION 1. WHITE SHIRTS AND WINGS Buy a couple of white t-shirts for you and your son to wear. You will need 2 of the exact same shirts for each of you. Order some wings of your favorite kind and have them delivered to your house. Instead of using napkins, wipe your hands on each other’s shirts as napkins. Don’t tell him what is happening - just reach over and wipe your hands on his shirt. This will create quite a mess, and he will want to wipe his hands on your shirt to get you back. Let him. Do this throughout the meal. When you are finished eating, talk about the way we hurt each other and stain each other in our relationships. Then wash your hands. Get 2 new white shirts and change into them. Then talk about how forgiveness is a beautiful act that gets rid of the relationship stains and lets us start over again. Make sure to clean up the mess, and make sure you get a couple of selfies in both sets of shirts. You may want to chat through this verse: Since God chose you to be the holy people he loves, you must clothe yourselves with tenderhearted mercy, kindness, humility, gentleness, and
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
